mean, only about 1% of our traffic comes from there, so I'm not simply worried about it right now, but just kind of understanding a bit where we are there.

Like, this is where we can ultimately, like, move really, really fast, produce high-quality content fast in a repeatable nature once we start to calibrate and understand your brand.
This isn't just about, like, creating content at scale for the sake of it.
It's just more trying to understand when we can find that repeatable quality, how can we move really, really fast and just, you know, change the game of what's possible in your engine.
So hopefully that gives you a little bit more of a flavor of kind of just, like, how the day-to-day of it can work.
Yeah, we're done.
And, Kyle, the way I saw it on the email, Will, was, you know, one of your goals is to also rank in these tools, right, like complexity, et cetera.
Like, we see what Google is doing, and we see there's this new thing coming up, and that has been one of the goals for certain customers, right, that see that horizon.
Yeah, thanks for the nudge on that.
Here's an example of, it could easily lead into a larger discussion of where are we actually at with optimizing for these tools, and there's so many hot takes that are out there.

And there's some things, certainly schema and article structure-wise that can help, but we find that through our process, when you're really understanding those questions and the right methodology, that we are finding traction in both LLMs and, in this case, perplexity in AI search, as well as Google search.
So here's one where the same article is ranking that we did these expense category pages for RAMP.
That's ranking first here.
Yes, there's AI answers, but this still actually gets quite healthy traffic, while also...

As you mentioned earlier, like, you know, still a relatively small percentage of what can come through here.
We are finding, though, like, you know, certainly a lot of our brands, our customers are getting traction with these tools.
You know, it's really engaging traffic, but it is relatively small traffic.
But it's something we're continuing to evolve, test around.
And my whole thing is, like, I don't want to be in the hot takes business with this stuff.
You know, I want to develop our own hypotheses and, like, really validate them and see that we have traction on this kind of stuff.
And so, you know, our take is if we are really mindful of those key questions associated to your personas, understand those at a deep level and how to create fantastic answers for that.
Now, are there other things around the edges we can optimize to increase likelihood if it goes in these tools?
Yes, but...
That if we first anchor on that, that is the most important thing here to ultimately like building something that's repeatable.
Now, I'm sure that will continue to change in the future as some of these tools evolve how they process this.
But, you know, we've studied a lot of the different tools that are out there to help you measure this.
And it's a bit challenging, you know, like, you know, there's, you know, the profounds, the scrunches of the world, et cetera.
Essentially, their methodology is like you take a set of different, you know, keywords, if you will, but really phrases.
And they'll just continually prompt these engines and then return the results back to you and to the relative rankings.
The challenge is, though, like depending on a variety of factors, small nuance and where you're pulling this data from, they pull via the API.
Or if you just change the slight structure of how you say that phrase, the results can be completely different.
And so, you know, it's something that we'll continue to bring into our practice.
But today, like a big.
Part of what we do is any client that has those tools will leverage them, but we're also looking at refer data through things like Google Analytics.
It's like, are you actually seeing direct traffic from this?
Now, that doesn't, there is a whole other complexity of like, are you mentioned, do people see that, and then do they go do a Google search or do they do something somewhere else?
And, you know, measuring that effect, of course, is going to be quite challenging.
But, yeah, something that's, you know, we hear more and more and more from our customers.
And, you know, it seems to have all the attention of the VCs today, and they're putting that pressure on their exec teams.
And what are you doing about this?
But, you know, in reality, the relative impact so far is not super huge.
And Kyle, was going to mention, you know, so there are three companies that we're working with, right?
One that's about to sign the contract, two of them are mid-market companies, but one of the three is actually a $15 million a year company.
And, you know, the reason why they signed up is because.
They weren't getting invited to the parties.
Essentially, their salespeople weren't getting invited to the RFPs, right?
So we're changing that, starting with content and then later distribution and conversion.
The first conversation we had with the CEO was actually quite funny because he's like, what matters to me is dollars.
It's revenue.
It's like, great.
We know that for sure.
But what else are you interested in that can take you to the revenue, right?
And I think out there, like three discovery calls, he understood that, okay, cool.
I got to give this a shot.
If I'm ever going to become a believer in it, well, we don't need to turn you into a believer, but, you know, different types of scope, right?
Obviously, the mid-market companies are investing twice as hard into the volume of the content, into what we're doing.
They want to do more.
So, you know, you're somewhere in the middle there, right?
I think there is a fit, but it's up to you, right, like to determine whether you have enough confidence in what we're doing.
One other thing, I'm trying to remember what else you referenced in the email.
You were mentioning just like highly regulated.
That isn't something that is unusual for us.
You know, we work with, you know, one, it's very different, but one company, Sunbit, that literally we are writing about things related to dentists and like the actual work they're doing.
And it has to be like reviewed by dentists to ensure like that is accurate.

We work with a lot of really technical brands, you know, the Zuplos of the world that are API management tool that like if you say anything wrong, those developers are going to sniff it out right away.
A prophecy that is, you know, data integration tool, GCAI, which is a lot, believe it or not, for like AI for lawyers.
And so like a variety of different like places where we've had to get deep and understand like what is it, what are the things we can say?
What's the approach to needing to validate that this is like, you know, true?
Really accurate content.
We have built a fact checker as a part of working with these brands because it's really important for us to like watch out for things like hallucinations.

We can also even point these to only reference things that are like, you know, we literally can like say you can only work from this universe of content that at times can be like a database of content from our customers.
And so therefore, like that helps to inform and like not go beyond the facts that we can interpret from there.

So there's a variety of different like things we've picked up along our journey of just like, hey, you know, we're going to have more and more complex, you know, content clients in a variety of different ways.
What that means for each industry is going to be different.
Like how can we support them so they feel confident in the quality content that we put out?
So, but I'd say like more often than not, that's becoming the norm for us at this point.
I was saying the early parts of us, our journey, it made me nervous.
I was like, how are we going to do this?
How are we going to make this repeatable?
Do we want to play in this space?
You go for easier plays where it's just more straightforward, and the team had a lot of conviction that we could build something that was repeatable, and they definitely were right.

You know, I realize there's some skepticism of, like, is organic worth your time to the, probably not the right way of phrasing it, but, you know, worth more of your time today?
Do you see any opportunities to us, like, really being able to enhance your team within either, it becomes a bit more interesting for you?
I think, yeah, my concerns are, I'm still not really clear what the inputs on the content would be, and how those would differentiate us from competitors.

Like, what would be the stop, we have a lot of plagiarism in our space, like, what would be one of our competitors putting similar inputs and similar prompts into the system, and similar limits on the content, and creating...

Very, very similar content on their websites relative to what we create, and if it relies on our experts kind of adding their own inputs in order to make that unique and add that value, then that's kind of basically what we're doing with updates anyway, and it's not really going to save a lot of time, and if it's still going to require kind of human in the loop to add all these inputs to update and build the richness in the content, then I'm just, I'm not sold on the originality.

I am concerned about accuracy and the need for humans to check accuracy, like we're a regulated industry, our clients go to prison, if they don't do what we do, it's pretty high stakes, and we do have clients, they shouldn't, but they use our books to help write their policies and guidance, so if we say the regulation says one thing and it actually does something else, that could be a challenge, and so then there's a lot of manual checking that still has to go on to make sure that it's interpreted that correctly.

All these regulations are in PDFs, I don't know if those are scannable in the same way as, like, there's just a lot of these kind of different, I'm just not, I'm not totally clear on the inputs, and my other concern is around cost.
I did have a look on your website.
I think it's about $9,000 a month, right, as a startup price.
That would make you twice as expensive as our CRM, so as HubSpot, which is our marketing automation platform, that would be the most expensive platform in marketing by 2x.
So I kind of have those two concerns, really.
One is the inputs in terms of building the detail.
The other one is the cost side of things.
We'd have to cancel probably our three most expensive marketing platforms to pay for this, and I'm not seeing that level of value, to be really honest.
Yeah.
How much are you paying in paid on a monthly basis?
Paid is a lot.
Paid is, like, I'm not sure on a monthly basis, but it's significant.
So I'm curious, why not apply that same logic to paid?
I'm not a...
Yeah, you have MQL targets that come...
The paid is based on cost per MQL, how much do we reach MQL.
That budget's been cut very significantly over the last couple of years.
that is.
Yeah, You
We ended up having to increase it again because those cuts have then impacted our pipeline.
I think it's more just being pragmatic that like paying for a tool like this would come out of a kind of the software side of the budget.
that's like, you don't have, you don't have a line item for today.
So you would have to find.
Yeah, and we're already being able to cut that further.
And like, yeah, HubSpot's the most expensive platform.
That's about 65,000 a year.
We're not going to take money out of paid when we're literally, if we have open headcount budget, it goes into paid until we have rehired people.
Like there's just, there's so, we're so limited.
So it's kind of those two things.
If it was like 5,000 a month, you know, it would be like a, or, you know, if there was some way of proving the concept and showing what it could do.

I think right now it's just, it feels a bit abstract and it feels a bit like I would worry about the amount of human time that we needed to A, get it up and running, B, kind of check that everything is accurate and whether it would just end up creating quite a lot of work versus kind of saving time.
That would be my concern.
Yeah, understood.

I would say related to the concerns about regulation and fact-checking and things of that nature, perhaps there are certain topics where there's less comfortability of us playing and we can not focus our time there.

I don't know how applicable that is for you in this case, but there are times where like, that reason, but it is not unusual for us to work with brands where it's like, okay, this is a lane where your more human-led content team motion will lead and we can help support them in a variety of ways, but they're going to more lead and will help enable them.
Where there's other parts where we can, we can take leave.

And, you know, I don't know in this case, if that's really like a value add and to address even what you're referring to in terms of like concerns around regulation and how that might be different on certain.

But, you know, this is another reason why we have a very, like, thorough fact-checking process that, like, is literally going to break down do we have sources that can highly, like, reference this with context, with confidence, rather.
And if we can't, it's flagged, and that's something we can go through and review, and through our calibration phase, we can continue to find that.

But in terms of, like, time and needed from your team, like, there's definitely time that's needed from your team, especially in the early going, especially through our calibration phase, because that just helps to educate our team and, like, how to think about, you know, see the varying patterns, right?
Like, whether that be tone, structure, or how we talk to your personas in a variety of different ways.
But as we find those patterns, what we're looking for is, like, how can we find repeatable ways of addressing that?
And once we start doing that through our inputs to what we do, that's what enables us to start to move fast and require less of your time.
So really how the high level...

Our process works is like, you know, in the first, you know, few weeks, we're going through that like collaboration and strategic development and calibration of our initial pieces that we're creating, getting that feedback.
And then by week four plus is usually when we start to publish and start to scale content.
But what that means for like highly regulated, more complex brands, sometimes we have to go a little bit slower.
Sometimes it may make sense to like only start scaling by like month or week six or so.
It's no hard, fast rule here.
It's just more like, hey, do we feel confident?
Okay, we've done two pieces.
Okay, like we've nailed these.
Okay, we've gone to four.
Are we feeling good?
All right, you know, let's start to continue to scale on where we feel like we're seeing repeatability.
So naturally, once we get humming and, you know, it's just more about like what topics are we talking about and why?
And, you know, what kind of opportunities do we see and getting your feedback, you know?
We meet weekly with our clients.
We have a lot of async comps.
big users of Slack and Notion.
And so we find we get in the rhythm where we just need less and less of the team's time.

But, you know, in terms of, you know, the relative ROI, what I would, you know, what I would say, you know, certainly had been in that place where, you know, an opportunity comes along and I don't have it in my budget and I, you know, understand that pain have had to go through that many times before.
You know, for me, the way I just think about it is, like, there's going to be a long-term pain of being, like, overly reliant on paid and, like, helping ensure you have that organic momentum.
Our take, obviously, you know, can be argued bias is going to be something that's going to pay off in the long term.

And, you know, while organic, you know, typical, like, short-term investment in SEO is not always going to hit those same MQL, cost per MQL, cost per opportunity as paid in, like, month three by month six plus and beyond.
It tends to be not even comparable in terms of like what you can get out of these channels, as I'm sure you've seen.
So, you know, that's the way we kind of think about like, hey, it's an investment that can be, you know, put to later.

Yeah, Bruno.
Yeah, well, I was just going to make a point and ask a question, right?

With organic, you're building that, right?

For example, that's an area we build a lot of traffic around through our webinars and things like that.
That's an area where we have a lot of retained audience that we can then nurture and engage.
I'm just not sure that.

Essentially doubling our organic spend right now is the right thing, and even if it was, I'm not going to get approval for a tool that is over 100k a year, without being able to show value in the short term.

It's not going to come out of paid right now.
We have very aggressive revenue goals for this year.
We know the paid side, at least for now, is working.
It is a short strategy, but we're a scale-up.
We have to think about getting to the end of the year right now.
We're not in the business of convincing customers.
For sure.
It's good to see.
I'm not sure about the value it would add relative to the cost for us right now.

I'm just not sold on where we are right now.
Fair enough.
Yeah, to Bruno's point, yeah, no, we don't.

[Explore potential partnership between ComplyAdvantage and GrowthX for AI-powered content creation and SEO optimization.](https://fathom.video/share/WGM8N2FDJWx7FzdvT1QWFvnYjfCWpu4j?tab=summary&timestamp=12.0)

  - [GrowthX offers AI-powered content workflows to scale high-quality content creation (8-16 pieces/week)](https://fathom.video/share/WGM8N2FDJWx7FzdvT1QWFvnYjfCWpu4j?tab=summary&timestamp=893.0)
  - [Cost ($9k/month) and concerns about AI-generated content quality/uniqueness are key barriers for ComplyAdvantage](https://fathom.video/share/WGM8N2FDJWx7FzdvT1QWFvnYjfCWpu4j?tab=summary&timestamp=2580.0)
  - [Not a fit currently due to budget constraints and ComplyAdvantage's focus on paid acquisition](https://fathom.video/share/WGM8N2FDJWx7FzdvT1QWFvnYjfCWpu4j?tab=summary&timestamp=2657.0)

## Topics
